text.title: NoSQL Patterns Examples
text.summary: End-to-end worked example illustrating the methodology against a realistic input.

example.title: MongoDB profile store + Redis session cache for a SaaS dashboard
example.note: Access patterns: get_user_profile_by_id (1200 QPS, p95 30ms); list_recent_orders (200 QPS, p95 100ms); cache_session_lookup (3500 QPS, p95 5ms). Store class: document for profile (single-aggregate read), key_value (Redis) for sessions. Embed verdict: preferences + last_5_addresses embedded (bounded), orders referenced (unbounded over time). TTL policy: `auth:session` 3600s; `cache:user-profile` 600s. Indexes: users.email + users.tenant_id. Partition key: tenant_id documented for future sharding. Spec passed validator, schema-validation enabled on `users` collection, no hot-partition risk because tenant_id distributes writes.

example.title: What goes wrong without this methodology
example.note: Team picked MongoDB by default; embedded `events` array on user document grew unbounded; document hit 16MB at 18-month mark; writes failed silently. No TTL on Redis prefix `user:cache:*`; maxmemory hit caused random evictions of session keys; users logged out. Decision tree would have caught both: embed without bound → reference; missing ttl → ttl-on-every-cache-key rule.
